column1: CLF-C02,"Domain 2: Security & Compliance","Security and Compliance","## CLF-C02 Domain 2: Security and Compliance
This domain tests your understanding of AWS security services, identity management, data protection, compliance programs, and how the Shared Responsibility Model applies to real scenarios.

column4: You chose IaaS; you manage the OS

column1: - ""Security OF the cloud"" = AWS responsibility (infrastructure, hardware, global network)
- ""Security IN the cloud"" = Customer responsibility (data, access, configuration, applications)

column4: Full access; NEVER use for daily tasks; enable MFA immediately
Attach policies to groups; simplifies management
No permanent credentials; uses temporary tokens; preferred for services
Attached to users, groups, or roles; defines Allow/Deny

column2: Don't use root for daily tasks

column3: No hardcoded keys; instance assumes role
Role in Account B trusted by Account A
External users assume role; no IAM user needed

column1: - Root account - ALWAYS (first thing on new account)
- IAM users with console access - Strongly recommended
- Privileged users - Required for admin-level access

column3: Single bill for all accounts; volume discounts
Group accounts by function (Prod, Dev, Security)
Guardrails limiting what accounts CAN do

column1: - SCPs are permission boundaries - they **restrict** what's possible in an account.
- SCPs do NOT grant permissions (IAM policies grant; SCPs limit).
- Applied to OUs or individual accounts.
- The management account is NOT affected by SCPs.
- Automates multi-account setup following best practices.
- Applies guardrails (preventive SCPs + detective Config rules).
- Provides a dashboard for compliance visibility.

## Key Exam Patterns for CLF-C02 Domain 2
- **""Single sign-on across accounts?""** -> IAM Identity Center
- **""SG vs. NACL?""** -> SG=stateful, allow only. NACL=stateless, allow+deny
- **30% of the exam** - spend the most study time here.
- **Shared Responsibility Model** appears in multiple questions. More managed = more AWS responsibility.
- **IAM Roles > IAM Users** for services. EC2/Lambda accessing other services = ALWAYS a Role.
- **Root user** should ONLY be used for account-level tasks. Everything else -> IAM users/roles.
- **Least privilege** = give only permissions needed. #1 IAM principle tested.
- **GuardDuty vs. Inspector vs. Macie:** GuardDuty=threats, Inspector=vulnerabilities, Macie=sensitive data.
- **CloudTrail vs. Config:** CloudTrail=""Who did what?"" Config=""What changed?""
- **KMS vs. CloudHSM:** KMS=managed, most use cases. CloudHSM=dedicated hardware, regulatory.
- **Secrets Manager vs. Parameter Store:** Secrets Manager=rotation, designed for secrets. Parameter Store=simpler, cheaper.
- **AWS Artifact** is ONLY for downloading compliance documents.
- **Security Hub** aggregates findings from GuardDuty, Inspector, Macie, Config into one dashboard.
- **SCPs don't grant permissions** - they only restrict. SCP Deny always wins over IAM Allow.
- **Encryption** is the customer's choice to enable. AWS provides tools; YOU turn it on.
WHAT DO YOU NEED?                        -> USE THIS
Find sensitive data in S3                -> Macie
See who made API calls                   -> CloudTrail
Manage security across accounts          -> Firewall Manager
Identify external sharing                -> IAM Access Analyzer
This covers all objectives in CLF-C02 Domain 2:
- **Shared Responsibility Model** - AWS vs. customer by service type
- **IAM** - Users, groups, roles, policies, least privilege
- **MFA** - Types, where to enable, root protection
- **Security services** - GuardDuty, Inspector, Macie, Detective, Security Hub
- **Data protection** - KMS, CloudHSM, ACM, Secrets Manager
- **Network security** - Security Groups, NACLs, WAF, Shield
- **Logging** - CloudTrail, Config, CloudWatch, VPC Flow Logs
